---
title: "Introduction to<br>R & RStudio"
#subtitle: "3rd SARA Summer School<br>22-26 July 2026"
#author: "Dr. Ajay Kumar Koli"
format: 
  revealjs:
    auto-animate: true
    theme: [default, style-sea-summer.scss, sara-your-turn.scss]
    slide-number: c/t
    logo: images/logo-sara.png
    transition: slide
    incremental: true
    scrollable: true
execute: 
  echo: true
#footer: "[Study Material Website](https://sara-course-r4b.netlify.app/)"  
title-slide-attributes: 
  data-background-image: "https://images.unsplash.com/vector-1746618662777-7058cb830c6a?q=80&w=1567&auto=format&fit=crop&ixlib=rb-4.1.0&ixid=M3wxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8fA%3D%3D"
---

## Dr. Ajay Kumar Koli, PhD {.center}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="70%"}

- Founder & Director of SARA
- JNU, Pondicherry Central University, University of Hyderabad, University of Würzbürg 
- Pursuing MSc Data Science from IGNOU

:::

::: {.column width="25%"}

![](images/speakers/ajay.png){fig-align="center"}

[{{< fa brands linkedin >}}](https://www.linkedin.com/in/ajay-kumar-koli/) [{{< fa brands github >}}](https://github.com/koliajaykr) [{{< fa brands twitter >}}](https://x.com/ajay_kolii) [{{< iconify fa6-brands bluesky >}}](https://bsky.app/profile/ajaykoli.bsky.social) [{{< fa brands instagram >}}](https://www.instagram.com/sara_data_sci/)

:::

:::

::: {.footer}
Slides prepared with 💙 & [Quarto](https://quarto.org/)
:::


## Data Science Project {background-color="black"}

<br>

![](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/diagrams/data-science/base.png){fig-align="center"}

::: footer
Image source: <https://r4ds.hadley.nz/>
:::

## The R Programming Language

<br> <br>

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
![](https://www.r-project.org/logo/Rlogo.png)
:::

::: {.column width="5%"}

:::

::: {.column width="70%"}
> "R is a free software environment for statistical computing and graphics."
:::
:::::

::: footer
R website link: <https://www.r-project.org/>
:::

## 🥳 Latest Ranking of R

Rank [`#8`]{.stat-callout} in May 2026: Highest Position (since 2007)

![](images/ratiing-r.png){fig-align="center"}

::: footer
Source: <https://www.tiobe.com/tiobe-index/r/>
:::

## History of R

:::: {.nonincremental}

- Intially as **S** language by Bell Labs.

- First appeared in **August 1993** as R language by:

::: {layout-ncol=2 }
![Ross Ihaka, New Zealand Statistician](images/ross.jpg){width=2.50in}

![Robert Gentleman, Canadian Statistician](images/robert.jpg){width=2.50in}
:::

::::

## Download R from CRAN {.clr-ocean background-color="black"}

Demo: <https://www.r-project.org/>

![](images/cran.png)

## R Console

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

-   R version 4.6.0 (2026-04-24)

-   R name "Because it was There"

-   R licence "ABSOLUTELY NO WARRANTY"

-   R prompt `>`
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

![](images/console-r.png){width=75% fig-align='center'}
:::
:::

## Workspace Image

:::: columns
::: {.column width="52%"}

<br>

[Don't save workspace image]{.badge}

-   It helps in "freshly minted R sessions".

-   "put more trust in your script than in your memory".
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
![](images/console-workspace.png){width=75% fig-align='center'}
:::
::::

::: footer
Source: [R Bloggers](https://www.r-bloggers.com/2017/04/using-r-dont-save-your-workspace/)
:::

# R Coding

## Ways to Run R code

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Console

- Type code directly after the `>` prompt and press Enter to run
- Best for quick, one-line commands and experimenting
- Output appears immediately below your command
- Nothing is saved - closing R loses everything you typed
- Think of it as a **scratch pad**

## R Script (`.R` File)

- Write multiple lines of code in a plain text file saved as `.R`
- Code is saved and reusable - you can share it with others
- Output appears in the Console, plots appear in the Plots pane


## Quarto File (`.qmd`)

- Mix R code chunks with text, headings, and images in one document
- Click `Render` to produce a final HTML, PDF, or Word report
- Perfect for reproducible research and sharing your full analysis as a report
:::

## R Console {auto-animate=true}

<br>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Code
```{r}
#| label: number
#| eval: false
7
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Output

```{r}
#| label: number
#| echo: false
```
:::
:::

. . .

::: {.tip-box}
Press `Enter` key to run the code in console

Copy code to clipboard
:::

## R Console: Addition {auto-animate=true}

<br>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Code]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: add
#| eval: false
2 + 1
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Output]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: add
#| echo: false

```
:::
:::

## Operators

<br>

`12 + 3` in this code [`+`]{.stat-callout} is an operator.

<br>

. . . 

> "Operators are used to perform operations on **variables** and **values.**"

::: footer
Source: [w3schools](https://www.w3schools.com/r/r_operators.asp)
:::

## Arithmetic Operators

> "Arithmetic operators are used with numeric values to perform common mathematical operations."

. . . 

::: {.tip-box}
- Put spaces between and around operators (`= + - * /`)

- Increase screen font size for better view.
:::

## Arithmetic Operators

<br>

| Operator | Name           | Example   |
|----------|----------------|-----------|
| `+`      | Addition       | `x + y`   |
| `-`      | Subtraction    | `x - y`   |
| `*`      | Multiplication | `x * y`   |
| `/`      | Division       | `x / y`   |
| `^`      | Exponent       | `x ^ y`   |
| `%%`     | Remainder      | `x %% y`  |

::: footer
Source: [w3schools](https://www.w3schools.com/r/r_operators.asp)
:::


## R Console: Subtraction {auto-animate=true}

<br>

::: columns

::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Code]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: subtract
#| eval: false
10 - 2
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Output]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: subtract
#| echo: false
```
:::

:::

## R Console: Multiplication {auto-animate=true}

<br>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Code]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: multiply
#| eval: false
12 * 4
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Output]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: multiply
#| echo: false
```
:::
:::

## R Console: Division {auto-animate=true}

<br>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Code]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: division
#| eval: false
25 / 5
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Output]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: division
#| echo: false
```
:::
:::

## R Console 

<br>

::: columns
::: {.column width="70%"}
### [Code]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: calci5
#| eval: false
3434 + 343453 * 2323 / 534 - 1000
```
:::

::: {.column width="30%"}
### [Output]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: calci5
#| echo: false
```
:::

::: {.tip-box}
R follows the BODMAS (bracket, order, division, multiplication, addition and subtraction) rule to solve mathematical equations.
:::
:::

## R Comparison Operators

<br>

> "Comparison operators are used to compare two values."

## R Comparison Operators

<br>

| Operator | Name                     | Example   |
|----------|--------------------------|-----------|
| `==`     | Equal                    | `x == y`  |
| `!=`     | Not equal                | `x != y`  |
| `>`      | Greater than             | `x > y`   |
| `<`      | Less than                | `x < y`   |
| `>=`     | Greater than or equal to | `x >= y`  |
| `<=`     | Less than or equal to    | `x <= y`  |

::: footer
Source: [w3schools](https://www.w3schools.com/r/r_operators.asp)
:::

## R Console

<br>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Code]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: logic1
#| eval: false
4 == 5
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Output]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: logic1
#| echo: false
```
:::
:::

## R Console

<br>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Code]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: logic2
#| eval: false
67 > 60
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Output]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: logic2
#| echo: false
```
:::
:::

## R Miscellaneous Operator {auto-animate=true}

> "Miscellaneous operators are used to manipulate data."

<br>

. . .

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Code]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: misc1
#| eval: false
12:36
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Output]{.center}

```{r}
#| label: misc1
#| echo: false
```

:::
:::

::: footer
Source: [w3schools](https://www.w3schools.com/r/r_operators.asp)
:::

## [What if we want to add all these values in R?]{.clr-tom}

<br>

```{r}
#| label: misc1
#| echo: false
```

# R Functions 

## R Function

> A function is a **shortcut for a task we do repeatedly**.

. . .

> "A function, in a programming environment, is a set of **instructions**."

## R Function `sum()` {auto-animate=true}

::: panel-tabset
### Code

```{r}
#| label: fun-sum1
#| eval: false
sum(2, 4, 6)
```

### Output

```{r}
#| label: fun-sum1
#| echo: false
```
:::

## Structure of a R function {.center transition="fade"}

<br>

![](images/function_str1.png)

## Structure of a R function {.center transition="fade"}

<br>

![](images/function_str2.png)

## Structure of a R function {.center transition="fade"}

<br>

![](images/function_str3.png)

## Structure of a R function {.center transition="fade"}

<br>

![](images/function_str4.png)

## Structure of a R function {.center transition="fade"}

<br>

![](images/function_str5.png)

<!-- ## Structure of R function {.center background-image="images/function-args.png" background-size="80%"} -->

## R Function `sum()`

::: panel-tabset
### Code

```{r}
#| label: fun-sum2
#| eval: false
sum(12:36)
```

### Output

```{r}
#| label: fun-sum2
#| echo: false
```
:::

## Round function {auto-animate=true}

::: panel-tabset
### Code

Function with default argument.

```{r}
#| label: fun-round2
#| eval: false
round(x = 42.58756)
```

or 

```{r}
#| label: fun-round22
#| eval: false
round(42.58756)
```

### Output

```{r}
#| label: fun-round2
#| echo: false
```

or

```{r}
#| label: fun-round22
#| echo: false
```
:::

## Round function

::: panel-tabset
### Code

Function with a specific value of an argument.

```{r}
#| label: fun-round3
#| eval: false
round(x = 46.58754, digits = 2)
```

or

```{r}
#| label: fun-round33
#| eval: false
round(46.58754, 2)
```

### Output

```{r}
#| label: fun-round3
#| echo: false
```

or

```{r}
#| label: fun-round33
#| echo: false
```
:::

## Square Root Function {auto-animate=true}

::: panel-tabset
### Code

Function with a specific value of an argument.

```{r}
#| label: fun-sqrt
#| eval: false
sqrt(x = 9)
```

### Output

```{r}
#| label: fun-sqrt
#| echo: false
```
:::

## Sequence Function {auto-animate=true}

::: panel-tabset
### Code

Function with a specific value of an argument.

```{r}
#| label: fun-seq
#| eval: false
seq.int(from = 10, to = 30, by = 5)
```

or

```{r}
#| label: fun-seq11
#| eval: false
seq.int(10, 30, 5)
```

### Output

```{r}
#| label: fun-seq
#| echo: false
```

or 

```{r}
#| label: fun-seq1
#| echo: false
```

or

```{r}
#| label: fun-seq11
#| echo: false
```

:::


## Plot using R {auto-animate=true}

::: panel-tabset
### Code

```{r}
#| label: plot1
#| eval: false
plot(1:100)
```

### Output

```{r}
#| label: plot1
#| echo: false
```
:::


## Getting Help with R

- The `help()` function and `?` help operator in R. 

- Use `args()` function to the get the list of arguments of a function.

::: footer
[R Website](https://www.r-project.org/help.html)
:::

## Your Turn {.your-turn}

```{r}
#| echo: false
library(countdown)

countdown(minutes = 15, play_sound = TRUE, top = 0)
```

::: {.nonincremental}
-  Add two numbers: 15 + 27

- Create a sequence of numbers from 1 to 10

- Create a sequence from 5 to 50, interval by 5

- Find the square root of 81

- Round the number 3.786 to 2 decimal places
:::

## Answers {.answer}

- Add two numbers: 15 + 27, `15 + 27`

- Create a sequence of numbers from 1 to 10, `seq(1, 10)`

- Create a sequence from 5 to 50, interval of 5, `seq(5, 50, by = 5)`

- Find the square root of 81, `sqrt(81)`

- Round the number 3.786 to 2 decimal places, `round(3.786, 2)`

## 😏 That's okay but how to ...

::: nonincremental
- **Everything in one Software** codes, results, writing.

- **Always reproducible** re-run work, anytime, by anyone.

- **Share and publish** with other Word, PDF or HTML.
:::

## {transition="zoom" transition-speed="slow" .center background-color="black"}

![](https://media3.giphy.com/media/o6FWop1Gbuyly/giphy.gif?cid=ecf05e474i92dhzes35zr0jtervmdixne1moeehvvtnntmm8&ep=v1_gifs_related&rid=giphy.gif&ct=g){fig-align="center" width=75%}

[We need a superhero 🐾 RStudio ]{.r-fit-text .badge}

# RStudio IDE 

## 

<br>
<br>
<br>

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="40%"}
![](images/logo_rstd.png)
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
RStudio is an integrated development environment (IDE) for R and Python from company [posit](https://posit.co/)
:::

::::

## R and RStudio {.centering}

<br>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Imagine R as a powerful engine

<br>

[{{< fa gear size=7x >}}]{style="color:gray"}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### and RStudio as a stylish car

<br>

[{{< fa car size=7x >}}]{style="color:#F9C74F"}
:::
:::

::: footer
Download RStudio: <https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop>
:::

## RStudio IDE {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd_ide.png){fig-align="center"}

##  [RStudio $\rightarrow$ Tools $\rightarrow$ Global Options]{.r-fit-text .center} {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd_options1.png){fig-align="center"}

## [RStudio $\rightarrow$ Tools $\rightarrow$ Global Options]{.r-fit-text .centering} {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd_options2.png){fig-align="center"}

## RStudio Without Project {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd-proj-none.png){fig-align=center}

## RStudio Without Project {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd-proj-folders.png){fig-align=center}

## RStudio Project Helps: {background-image="images/r-projects.png" background-size="55%" background-position="bottom right"}

::: columns
::: {.column width="100%"}
"to divide your work into multiple contexts, each with their own"

- Working directory,
- Workspace,
- History, and
- Source documents.

:::
:::

::: footer
Source: [RStudio](https://support.rstudio.com/hc/en-us/articles/200526207-Using-Projects) & artwork by [Allison Horst](https://www.allisonhorst.com/)
:::

## Create RStudio project {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd_proj1.png){fig-align=center}

## Create RStudio project {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd_proj2.png){fig-align=center}

## In case anything goes wrong$...$ {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd_proj3.1.png){fig-align=center}

## Create RStudio Project {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd_proj3.png){fig-align=center}

## Create RStudio project {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd_proj4.png){fig-align=center}

## Create RStudio project {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd_proj4a.png){fig-align=center}

## Create RStudio project {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd_proj4b.png){fig-align=center}

## Create RStudio project {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd_proj4c.png){fig-align=center}

## RStudio project "name" {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd_proj_name.png){fig-align=center}

## RStudio project "path" {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd_proj_path1.png){fig-align=center}

## RStudio project {background-color=black}

![](images/rstd_proj_path2.png){fig-align=center}

## Your Turn {.your-turn}



::: {.task-box}
### Run these codes in the console of RStudio
- "Hello World"
- 12 + 2
- 3 * 9
- 34 == 34
- 67 == 90
:::

# R Script

## Pros of R Script (`.R` File) {.scrollable}

- **Clean and focused** - a `.R` file contains only code, making it easy to read, edit, and run without any extra formatting.

- **Fast to execute** - you can run the entire script in one go, making it ideal data cleaning scripts, and automation.

- **Easy to share and version control** - a plain `.R` file is lightweight and it opens immediately.

## Create R Script {background-color="black"}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=30%}

![](images/r-script.png)

:::

::: {.column width=70%}

![](images/r-script-file.png)

<!-- [{{< fa file-arrow-down >}} Save "Five Essentials" Practice File (.R)](demo/five_essentials.R) -->

:::
:::

## How to use this file

- [Run one line at a time:]{.highlight} place cursor on a line: `Ctrl + Enter` (Win) / `Cmd + Enter` (Mac)
- [Run a whole section:]{.highlight} select lines → `Ctrl + Enter`
- [Run the full file:]{.highlight} `Ctrl + Shift + Enter`
- Output appears in the Console

## Variables & Data Types {auto-animate=true}

- A variable stores a value, like a box, so you can reuse it.

- Use  `<-`  to assign (R's way of saying "equals"): `Alt + -` (Win) / `option + -` (Mac)

- Character  (text - always in quotes), numeric (whole number or decimal) and logical (TRUE or FALSE)

## Create Variables {auto-animate=true}

```{r}
name <- "SARA Institute" # character

year <- 2023 # numeric

fee <- 4500.00 # numeric

is_free <- FALSE # logical
```

. . .

::: {.tip-box}
Comment is any line in R that starts with `#` - R completely ignores it when running code
:::

## Print a Variable {auto-animate=true}

Type variable name and run

::: {.columns}

::: {.column}

### Code

```{r}
#| label: print-vars
#| eval: false
name

#year

fee

#is_free
```

:::

::: {.column}

### Output

```{r}
#| label: print-vars
#| echo: false
```

:::

:::


## Type of a Variable {auto-animate=true}

Type variable name and run

::: {.columns}

::: {.column}

### Code

```{r}
#| label: type-vars
#| eval: false
class(name)

class(year)

class(fee)

class(is_free)
```

:::

::: {.column}

### Output

```{r}
#| label: type-vars
#| echo: false
```

:::
:::

## See a quick Summary of Variable {auto-animate=true}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column}

### Code

```{r}
#| label: str-vars
#| eval: false
str(name)

str(year)
```

:::

::: {.column}

### Output

```{r}
#| label: str-vars
#| echo: false
```

:::
:::

## Your Turn {.your-turn}

1. Create a variable called my_name with your name.

1. Create a variable called my_city with your city.

1. Print both. What class are they?

```{r}
#| echo: false
countdown(minutes = 10, play_sound = TRUE, top = 0)
```

## Beautiful R Codes {.scrollable}

::: {.tip-box}

- Writing readable code because other people might need to use your code.

- Writing readable code because you might need to use your code, a few weeks/months/years after you’ve written it.

- Put spaces between and around variable names and operators (`= + - * /`).

- Break up long lines of code.

- Keeping a consistent style.

:::

## Vector {auto-animate=true}

- A vector is a sequence of values of the SAME type.

- `c()` function means "combine" - it is how to build a vector.

. . .

```{r}
students <- c("Priya", "Rahul", "Anita", "Suresh", "Meena")

scores <- c(88, 75, 92, 60, 85)

passed <- c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)
```

## Print Vector {auto-animate=true}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=30%}

### Code

```{r}
#| label: print-vectors
#| eval: false
students

scores

passed
```

:::

::: {.column width=70%}

### Output

```{r}
#| label: print-vectors
#| echo: false
```

:::
:::

## Access Individual Elements {auto-animate=true}

R starts counting from 1

::: {.columns}

::: {.column}

### Code

```{r}
#| label: print-elements
#| eval: false
# first student
students[1]

# third score
scores[3]

# elements 2 to 4
students[2:4]

# elements 1 and 5
scores[c(1, 5)]
```

:::

::: {.column}

### Output

```{r}
#| label: print-elements
#| echo: false
```

:::
:::

## Arithmetic on Vector {auto-animate=true}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column}

### Code

```{r}
#| label: arith-vectors
#| eval: false
# add 5 bonus marks to everyone
scores + 5

# increase all scores by 10%
scores * 1.1

# TRUE/FALSE — who scored 70 or above?
scores >= 70
```

:::

::: {.column}

### Output

```{r}
#| label: arith-vectors
#| echo: false
```

:::
:::

## Built-in Summary Functions

- `length(scores)` for how many elements?
- `sum(scores)` for total
- `mean(scores)` for average
- `median(scores)` for middle value
- `max(scores)` for highest
- `min(scores)` for lowest
- `range(scores)` for min and max together
- `sd(scores)`for standard deviation

## Cons of R Script (`.R` File) {.scrollable}

- **Output is not saved with the code** - plots appear in the Plots pane and results in the Console, but closing RStudio loses everything; you have to rerun the script every time to see the output again.

- **No narrative or context** - you can only add short comments with `#`; there is no space to explain your thinking, interpret results, or present findings in a readable way for others.

- **Not reproducible as a report** — a `.R` file cannot be directly shared as a document; turning your analysis into something a non-coder can read requires extra steps, unlike `.qmd` which renders everything in one click.

## Your Turn {.your-turn}

1. Create a vector of 5 temperatures (any values you like).

1. Find the mean, max, and min.

1. Which temperatures are above 30? (use >= 30)

## Data Frames {background-color="black"}

![](https://cdn.myportfolio.com/45214904-6a61-4e23-98d6-b140f8654a40/85520b8f-4629-4763-8a2a-9ceff27458bf_rw_1920.jpg?h=21007b20ac00cf37318dca645c215453)

::: footer
Source: <https://allisonhorst.com/other-r-fun>
:::

## Data Frame {auto-animate=true}

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Code

```{r}
#| label: data-frame
#| eval: false
participants <- data.frame(
  name = c("Priya", "Rahul", "Anita", "Suresh", "Meena"),
  city = c("Delhi", "Jaipur", "Mumbai", "Lucknow", "Pune"),
  score = c(88, 75, 92, 60, 85),
  passed = c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)
)

# print the whole table
participants
```

## Output

```{r}
#| label: data-frame
#| echo: false
```

:::

## Look at the data frame

- `head(participants)` first 6 rows (useful for big data)

- `tail(participants)` last 6 rows

- `nrow(participants)` number of rows

- `ncol(participants)`  number of columns

- `dim(participants)`  rows and columns together

- `names(participants)`  column names

- `str(participants)`  structure — types of each column

- `summary(participants)`  quick stats for each column


## Access columns using `$` {auto-animate=true}

— gives you the whole column as a vector

<br>

```{r}
participants$name

participants$score
```

## Access a specific `[row, column]` {auto-animate=true}

```{r}
participants[1, ] # first row, all columns
participants[, 3] # all rows, third column (score)
participants[2, 3] # row 2, column 3 → Rahul's score
```

## Filter rows - who passed? {auto-animate=true}

```{r}
participants[participants$passed == TRUE, ]
```

## Who scored above 80? {auto-animate=true}

```{r}
participants[participants$score > 80, ]
```

## Add a new column {auto-animate=true}

```{r}
participants$grade <- ifelse(participants$score >= 80, "Distinction", "Pass")
participants
```

## Your Turn {.your-turn}

::: {.task-box}
- Create a data frame with 4 of your friends: `name`, `age`, and `favourite_subject`.
- Print only the `name` and `favourite_subject` columns.
- Filter: who is older than `25`?
:::

# R Packages

## R Packages 📦 {background-image="images/packages.png" background-position="bottom right" background-size="40%"}

> "An R package is a collection of **functions, data, and documentation** that extends the capabilities of base R. Using packages is key to the successful use of R."

::: footer
Source: [R for Data Science, 2e](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/intro)
:::

## R Packages

- Install the R package

- Call the R package

- Update the R package

- Remove the R package

## Install Packages {background-color="black"}

![](images/pkg-download1.png){fig-align="center"}

## Name of the Packages {background-color="black"}

![](images/pkg-download2.png){fig-align="center"}

## Installed Packages {background-color="black"}

![](images/pkg-download3.png){fig-align="center"}

## Tools $\rightarrow$ Package Updates {background-color="black"}

![](images/update-pkg1.png){fig-align="center"}

## Select Packages to Update {background-color="black"}

![](images/update-pkg2.png){fig-align="center"}

## Click Install Updates {background-color="black"}

![](images/update-pkg3.png){fig-align="center"}

## To Remove Packages {background-color="black"}

![](images/remove-pkg.png){fig-align="center"}

## Functions for Packages

<br>

::: {.columns}

::: {.column}
### Install a Package
We install a package once
```{r}
#| eval: false
install.packages("tidyverse")
```

:::

::: {.column}
### Call a Package

Call it atleast once in file
```{r}
#| eval: false
library(tidyverse)
```

:::

:::

. . .

::: {.tip-box}
If you un-install R or RStudio, you will lose all installed packages.
:::

## Create your own R Functions 

- A function is a reusable block of code that does one job.
- We have already used built-in functions: `mean()`, `sum()`, `c()`

## Basic structure of Function

<br>

```markdown
my_function <- function(argument1, argument2) {
    result <- code that does something
    return(result)
}
```

## Write a R Function {auto-animate=true}

<br>

```{r}
add_numbers <- function(x, y) {
  result <- x + y
  return(result)
}
```

. . .

1. [function name]{.badge} - what we call it `add_numbers()`
1. [An argument]{.badge} - what you give it `function(x, y)`
1. [A body]{.badge} - what it does with it `{result <- x + y` & `return(result)}`

## Example 1 - Add two numbers {auto-animate=true}

```{r}
add_numbers <- function(x, y) {
  result <- x + y
  return(result)
}

add_numbers(1, 2)

add_numbers(3, 55)

add_numbers(-3, 10)
```

## Example 2 - Greet a participant {auto-animate=true}

```{r}
greet <- function(name) {
  message <- paste("Hello,", name, "!")
  return(message)
}

greet("Kiran")

greet("Ajay")
```

## Example 3 - Convert Marks to Percent {auto-animate=true}

```{r}
to_percent <- function(marks, total = 100) {
  # total has a default value
  percent <- (marks / total) * 100
  return(round(percent, 2)) # round to 2 decimal places
}

to_percent(75) # out of 100 (default)
to_percent(55, total = 60) # out of 60
```

## Example 4 — Classify a Score {.scrollable auto-animate=true} 

```{r}
classify_score <- function(score) {
  if (score >= 90) {
    return("Excellent")
  } else if (score >= 75) {
    return("Good")
  } else if (score >= 60) {
    return("Pass")
  } else {
    return("Needs Improvement")
  }
}

classify_score(92)
classify_score(78)
classify_score(55)
```

## Your Turn {.your-turn}

::: {.task-box}
- Write a function called celsius_to_fahrenheit that converts temperature: `F = (C * 9/5) + 32`.
- Test it with 0, 100, and 37 (body temperature)
:::

## Answers {.answer}

```{r}
celsius_to_fahrenheit <- function(x) {
  F <- (x * 9 / 5) + 32
  return(F)
}

celsius_to_fahrenheit(37)
```

# Quarto

## Quarto {background-image="https://cdn.myportfolio.com/45214904-6a61-4e23-98d6-b140f8654a40/b041d35c-5117-4dd0-9b08-d2d0f7a77f9a.png?h=21528b7e9f1a2e24c9ec3d46cfca8653" background-size="65%" background-position="bottom"}

> "An open-source scientific and technical publishing system"

::: {.footer}
Source: <https://quarto.org/> | Artwork from [Allison Horst](https://allisonhorst.com/horst-hill-collaborations)
:::

## Quarto Workflow {background-color="black"}

<br>

![](https://cdn.myportfolio.com/45214904-6a61-4e23-98d6-b140f8654a40/b5217f2a-f129-4bf9-90dc-c5b9783d0ea8.png?h=f8d3ed40353f4ae6eb77dae39072510c)

## Create Quarto Document {background-color="black"}

::: {.centering layout-ncol=2 layout-valign="bottom"}

![Step 1](images/quarto-new-file.png){width="45%"}

![Step 2](images/quarto-new-document.png)

:::

## Quarto Main Components {auto-animate=true}

1. [YAML]{.badge}

1. [Markdown Text]{.badge}

1. [Code Chunk/Block/Cell]{.badge}

## YAML {auto-animate=true}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

```yaml
---
title: "Hello World"
subtitle: "2nd SARA Bootcamp 2026"
author: "Dr. Ajay Kumar Koli"
date: today
format: html
---

```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{=html}
<iframe src="./_example1.html" width="100%" height="500"></iframe>
```

:::

::::

::: footer
More YAML options for HTML ouput <https://quarto.org/docs/reference/formats/html.html>
:::

## Markdown Syntax {auto-animate=true}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

```yaml
---
title: "Hello World"
subtitle: "2nd SARA Bootcamp 2026"
author: "Dr. Ajay Kumar Koli"
date: today
format: html
---

## First Time Coding

- Relax

- Patience

## Having Fun

This is *just* the beginning. 
It will be **awesome** at the end. 
Learn more from this link <https://quarto.org/docs/reference/formats/html.html>.

## Add Image

![](images/rose.jpg)

```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{=html}
<iframe src="./_example-02.html" width="100%" height="500"></iframe>
```

:::

::::

::: footer
Markdown basics <https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html>
:::

## Code Blocks {auto-animate=true} 

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="70%"}

````yaml
---
title: "ggplot2 demo"
author: "Norah Jones"
date: "5/22/2021"
format: 
  html:
    code-fold: true
---

## Air Quality

@fig-airquality further explores the impact of temperature on ozone level.

```{{r}}
#| label: fig-airquality
#| fig-cap: "Temperature and ozone level."
#| warning: false

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(airquality, aes(Temp, Ozone)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method = "loess")
```
````

:::

::: {.column width="30%"}

<iframe src="./_example-03.html" width="100%" height="500"></iframe>

:::

::::


::: footer
R Code Blocks <https://quarto.org/docs/computations/r.html>
:::

## Inline Code {auto-animate=true}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="70%"}

````yaml
---
title: "Inline Coding"
date: "3/14/2026"
format: html
---

```{{r}}
marks <- c(2, 4, 6, 8)
```

The sum of the marks is `{{r}} sum(marks)`. 

The mean of the marks is `{{r}} mean(marks)`. 
````
:::

::: {.column width="30%"}

<iframe src="./_example-04.html" width="100%" height="500"></iframe>

:::

::::

## Quarto Output Formats

::: {.panel-tabset}
### HTML

```yaml
---
title: "My document"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    html-math-method: katex
    css: styles.css
---
```

### PDF

```yaml
---
title: "My document"
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
---
```

### WORD

```yaml
---
title: "My Document"
format:
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    highlight-style: github
---
```
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-caution}
Not all YAML options are supported across every output format.
:::

::: footer
More info <https://quarto.org/docs/output-formats/all-formats.html>
:::

## Thank you! {.center}

**Module 1 complete** 🎉

*SARA Institute of Data Science - 3rd SARA Summer School 22-26 July 2026 "R for Researchers"*

::: {.footer}
Course Slides website: <https://sara-course-r4b.netlify.app/>
:::
